concepts.name: Group 15 Elements – General Introduction
concepts.explanation: Group 15 elements (N, P, As, Sb, Bi) have the general electronic configuration ns²np³. They show a shift from non-metallic to metallic character down the group: N and P are non-metals, As and Sb are metalloids, and Bi is a typical metal.

concepts.name: Group 15 – Electronic Configuration and Occurrence
concepts.explanation: The valence shell electronic configuration is ns²np³. Nitrogen comprises 78% of the atmosphere and occurs as NaNO₃ (Chile saltpetre) and KNO₃. Phosphorus occurs in apatite minerals [Ca₉(PO₄)₆·CaX₂] and is essential for living organisms.

concepts.name: Group 15 – Atomic and Ionic Radii
concepts.explanation: Atomic and ionic radii increase down the group. There is a considerable increase from N to P, but from As to Bi only a small increase is observed due to the presence of completely filled d- and/or f-orbitals in heavier members (poor shielding).

concepts.name: Group 15 – Ionisation Enthalpy and Electronegativity
concepts.explanation: Ionisation enthalpy decreases down the group due to increasing atomic size. It is much greater than that of group 14 elements due to the extra stable half-filled p³ configuration. Electronegativity generally decreases down the group.

concepts.name: Group 15 – Oxidation States and Inert Pair Effect
concepts.explanation: Common oxidation states are -3, +3, and +5. The -3 state decreases down the group (Bi hardly shows it). +5 stability decreases down the group; +3 stability increases due to the inert pair effect. Bi(V) is only stable in BiF₅.

concepts.name: Anomalous Behaviour of Nitrogen
concepts.explanation: Nitrogen is anomalous due to its small size, high electronegativity, high ionisation enthalpy, and absence of d-orbitals. It forms strong pπ-pπ multiple bonds (N≡N), exists as a diatomic molecule, and has a very high bond enthalpy (941.4 kJ mol⁻¹).

concepts.name: Group 15 – Hydrides (EH₃)
concepts.explanation: Group 15 elements form hydrides EH₃ (NH₃, PH₃, AsH₃, SbH₃, BiH₃). Stability decreases down the group; reducing character increases. Basicity decreases: NH₃ > PH₃ > AsH₃ > SbH₃ > BiH₃. Bond angle decreases from NH₃ (107.8°) to BiH₃ (~91°).

concepts.name: Group 15 – Oxides and Halides
concepts.explanation: Oxides: E₂O₃ and E₂O₅. Acidity decreases down the group; N₂O₃ and P₂O₃ are acidic, As₂O₃ and Sb₂O₃ are amphoteric, Bi₂O₃ is basic. Halides: EX₃ and EX₅. N does not form pentahalides (no d-orbitals). Pentahalides are more covalent than trihalides.

concepts.name: Dinitrogen (N₂) – Preparation
concepts.explanation: N₂ is prepared industrially by fractional distillation of liquid air (b.p. N₂ = 77.2 K, O₂ = 90 K). In the laboratory: (i) NH₄Cl + NaNO₂ → N₂ + 2H₂O + NaCl; (ii) thermal decomposition of NH₄Cr₂O₇; (iii) thermal decomposition of NaN₃: 2NaN₃ → 2Na + 3N₂ (very pure).
concepts.formula_reaction: NH₄Cl + NaNO₂ → N₂ + 2H₂O + NaCl; (NH₄)₂Cr₂O₇ → N₂ + 4H₂O + Cr₂O₃

concepts.name: Dinitrogen – Properties and Uses
concepts.explanation: N₂ is colourless, odourless, non-toxic, and inert at room temperature due to high N≡N bond enthalpy. At high temperatures, it reacts with metals to form nitrides (Li₃N, Mg₃N₂) and non-metals. Uses: ammonia manufacture, inert atmosphere, refrigerant (liquid N₂).

concepts.name: Ammonia (NH₃) – Preparation (Haber's Process)
concepts.explanation: Ammonia is manufactured by Haber's process: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ (ΔH = -46.1 kJ mol⁻¹). Conditions: 200 atm pressure, ~700 K temperature, and iron catalyst with K₂O and Al₂O₃ promoters. Le Chatelier's principle favours high pressure and low temperature, but low temperature slows the rate.

concepts.name: Ammonia – Properties and Reactions
concepts.explanation: NH₃ is a colourless gas with a pungent odour. It is trigonal pyramidal with one lone pair, making it a Lewis base. It is highly soluble in water, forming a weakly basic solution: NH₃ + H₂O ⇌ NH₄⁺ + OH⁻. It forms ammonium salts and complexes with metal ions (e.g., [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺).

concepts.name: Oxides of Nitrogen
concepts.explanation: Nitrogen forms oxides in all oxidation states from +1 to +5: N₂O (+1, neutral), NO (+2, neutral), N₂O₃ (+3, acidic), NO₂ (+4, acidic), N₂O₄ (+4, acidic), N₂O₅ (+5, acidic). NO₂ dimerises to N₂O₄ due to odd electrons.

concepts.name: Nitric Acid (HNO₃) – Preparation (Ostwald's Process)
concepts.explanation: HNO₃ is manufactured by Ostwald's process: (1) 4NH₃ + 5O₂ → 4NO + 6H₂O (Pt/Rh catalyst, 500 K); (2) 2NO + O₂ → 2NO₂; (3) 3NO₂ + H₂O → 2HNO₃ + NO. The NO is recycled. HNO₃ can be concentrated to ~68% by distillation.

concepts.name: Nitric Acid – Properties and Reactions
concepts.explanation: HNO₃ is a colourless liquid, a strong acid, and a powerful oxidising agent. Cu with dilute HNO₃ gives NO; with concentrated HNO₃ gives NO₂. Zn with dilute HNO₃ gives N₂O; with concentrated gives NO₂. Cr and Al are passivated by concentrated HNO₃ (oxide film).

concepts.name: Brown Ring Test (Nitrate Test)
concepts.explanation: The brown ring test detects nitrate ions. Fe²⁺ reduces NO₃⁻ to NO, which reacts with Fe²⁺ to form a brown complex [Fe(H₂O)₅NO]²⁺. A brown ring forms at the interface between solution and conc. H₂SO₄.

concepts.name: Allotropes of Phosphorus – White Phosphorus
concepts.explanation: White phosphorus is a translucent, white waxy solid with P₄ tetrahedral molecules (bond angle 60°, highly strained). It is poisonous, insoluble in water, soluble in CS₂, glows in the dark (chemiluminescence). It is very reactive and catches fire in air to give P₄O₁₀.

concepts.name: Allotropes of Phosphorus – Red and Black Phosphorus
concepts.explanation: Red phosphorus is obtained by heating white phosphorus at 573 K in an inert atmosphere. It is polymeric, odourless, non-poisonous, insoluble in CS₂, and less reactive. Black phosphorus is the most stable form, obtained by heating red phosphorus under high pressure.

concepts.name: Phosphine (PH₃) – Preparation and Properties
concepts.explanation: PH₃ is prepared by: (i) Ca₃P₂ + 6H₂O → 3Ca(OH)₂ + 2PH₃; (ii) P₄ + 3NaOH + 3H₂O → PH₃ + 3NaH₂PO₂ (inert atmosphere). It is a colourless gas with a rotten fish smell, highly poisonous, and spontaneously flammable. It is weakly basic and gives phosphonium salts (PH₄I).

concepts.name: Phosphorus Trichloride (PCl₃) – Preparation and Properties
concepts.explanation: PCl₃ is prepared by passing dry Cl₂ over heated white phosphorus: P₄ + 6Cl₂ → 4PCl₃. It is a colourless oily liquid that hydrolyses in moisture: PCl₃ + 3H₂O → H₃PO₃ + 3HCl. It is pyramidal with phosphorus sp³ hybridised.

concepts.name: Phosphorus Pentachloride (PCl₅) – Preparation and Properties
concepts.explanation: PCl₅ is prepared by the reaction of white phosphorus with excess Cl₂: P₄ + 10Cl₂ → 4PCl₅. It is a yellowish white powder that hydrolyses: PCl₅ + H₂O → POCl₃ + 2HCl; POCl₃ + 3H₂O → H₃PO₄ + 3HCl. It is trigonal bipyramidal (axial bonds longer than equatorial). In the solid state, it is [PCl₄]⁺[PCl₆]⁻.

concepts.name: Oxoacids of Phosphorus
concepts.explanation: Phosphorus forms many oxoacids. Important ones include: H₃PO₂ (hypophosphorous acid, +1), H₃PO₃ (phosphorous acid, +3), H₄P₂O₆ (hypophosphoric acid, +4), H₃PO₄ (phosphoric acid, +5), H₄P₂O₇ (pyrophosphoric acid, +5), (HPO₃)ₙ (metaphosphoric acid, +5). Basicity equals the number of P-OH bonds. Acids with P-H bonds are good reducing agents.

concepts.name: Group 16 Elements – General Introduction
concepts.explanation: Group 16 elements (O, S, Se, Te, Po) are called chalcogens. They have the general electronic configuration ns²np⁴. Oxygen is the most abundant element on earth (46.6% of crust). Sulphur is found as sulphates and sulphides. Se and Te occur in sulphide ores.

concepts.name: Group 16 – Atomic and Ionic Radii, Ionisation Enthalpy
concepts.explanation: Atomic and ionic radii increase down the group. Oxygen is exceptionally small. Ionisation enthalpy decreases down the group but is lower than group 15 elements (due to less stable p⁴ configuration). Electron gain enthalpy: oxygen is less negative than sulphur due to small size.

concepts.name: Group 16 – Oxidation States
concepts.explanation: Group 16 elements show -2, +2, +4, and +6 oxidation states. The stability of -2 decreases down the group. +4 and +6 are common for S, Se, Te. +6 stability decreases down the group; +4 stability increases (inert pair effect). Oxygen shows -2 (except in OF₂ where it is +2).

concepts.name: Anomalous Behaviour of Oxygen
concepts.explanation: Oxygen is anomalous due to its small size and high electronegativity. It forms strong hydrogen bonds (H₂O vs H₂S). It does not have d-orbitals, limiting its covalency to four (rarely exceeds two). It forms pπ-pπ multiple bonds.

concepts.name: Group 16 – Hydrides (H₂E)
concepts.explanation: Group 16 elements form hydrides H₂O, H₂S, H₂Se, H₂Te, H₂Po. Acidic character increases down the group (H₂O < H₂S < H₂Se < H₂Te). Thermal stability decreases down the group. Reducing character increases from H₂S to H₂Te. H₂O is liquid due to H-bonding; others are gases.

concepts.name: Dioxygen (O₂) – Preparation
concepts.explanation: In the laboratory: (i) heating KClO₃ with MnO₂: 2KClO₃ → 2KCl + 3O₂; (ii) thermal decomposition of metal oxides (Ag₂O, Pb₃O₄, HgO); (iii) decomposition of H₂O₂. Industrially: fractional distillation of liquid air or electrolysis of water.

concepts.name: Dioxygen – Properties and Reactions
concepts.explanation: O₂ is a colourless, odourless gas (b.p. 90 K). It is paramagnetic due to two unpaired electrons in π* orbitals. It reacts with nearly all metals and non-metals except Au, Pt, and noble gases. Reactions are often exothermic but require initiation.

concepts.name: Simple Oxides – Classification
concepts.explanation: Oxides are classified as: (i) Acidic – non-metal oxides that give acids with water (SO₂, CO₂, N₂O₅); (ii) Basic – metal oxides that give bases with water (Na₂O, CaO, BaO); (iii) Amphoteric – react with both acids and alkalies (Al₂O₃, ZnO); (iv) Neutral – neither acidic nor basic (CO, NO, N₂O).

concepts.name: Ozone (O₃) – Preparation and Properties
concepts.explanation: Ozone is prepared by passing oxygen through a silent electrical discharge: 3O₂ → 2O₃ (ΔH = +142 kJ mol⁻¹). It is a pale blue gas, dark blue liquid, and violet-black solid. It is a powerful oxidising agent (nascent oxygen). It oxidises PbS to PbSO₄ and I⁻ to I₂.
concepts.formula_reaction: 3O₂ → 2O₃; O₃ + 2I⁻ + H₂O → 2OH⁻ + I₂ + O₂; PbS + 4O₃ → PbSO₄ + 4O₂

concepts.name: Ozone – Structure and Uses
concepts.explanation: Ozone is a resonance hybrid of two structures with identical O-O bond lengths (128 pm) and a bent shape (bond angle ~117°). It is used as a germicide, disinfectant, bleach, and oxidising agent. The ozone layer in the stratosphere absorbs harmful UV radiation.

concepts.name: Allotropes of Sulphur – Rhombic and Monoclinic
concepts.explanation: Rhombic sulphur (α-sulphur) is the stable form at room temperature (yellow, m.p. 385.8 K, density 2.06). Monoclinic sulphur (β-sulphur) is stable above 369 K (m.p. 393 K, density 1.98). Both have S₈ puckered rings. At ~1000 K, S₂ is the dominant species (paramagnetic).

concepts.name: Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂) – Preparation and Properties
concepts.explanation: SO₂ is prepared by: (i) S + O₂ → SO₂; (ii) laboratory: SO₃²⁻ + 2H⁺ → H₂O + SO₂; (iii) roasting of sulphide ores: 4FeS₂ + 11O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃ + 8SO₂. It is a colourless gas with a pungent smell, highly soluble in water, forming H₂SO₃. It is a reducing agent and decolourises KMnO₄.

concepts.name: Sulphur Dioxide – Structure and Uses
concepts.explanation: SO₂ is a resonance hybrid of two canonical forms with equal S-O bonds. It is angular (bent). Uses: refining petroleum and sugar, bleaching wool and silk, as an anti-chlor, disinfectant, and preservative. It is used in the manufacture of H₂SO₄.

concepts.name: Oxoacids of Sulphur – Structures
concepts.explanation: Sulphur forms several oxoacids: H₂SO₃ (sulphurous), H₂S₂O₃ (thiosulphuric), H₂S₂O₄ (dithionous), H₂S₂O₅ (disulphurous), H₂SₓO₆ (polythionic), H₂SO₄ (sulphuric), H₂S₂O₇ (pyrosulphuric), H₂S₂O₈ (peroxodisulphuric). Structures show central S atoms with S=O, S-OH, and S-S bonds.

concepts.name: Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄) – Contact Process
concepts.explanation: H₂SO₄ is manufactured by the Contact Process: (1) burning S or sulphide ores to produce SO₂; (2) catalytic oxidation: 2SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2SO₃ (V₂O₅ catalyst, 720 K, 2 bar); (3) absorption of SO₃ in H₂SO₄ to give oleum (H₂S₂O₇); (4) dilution of oleum with water to give H₂SO₄.
concepts.formula_reaction: 2SO₂ + O₂ → 2SO₃ (V₂O₅); SO₃ + H₂SO₄ → H₂S₂O₇; H₂S₂O₇ + H₂O → 2H₂SO₄

concepts.name: Sulphuric Acid – Properties and Reactions
concepts.explanation: H₂SO₄ is a colourless, viscous liquid (b.p. 611 K). It is a strong acid (ionises in two steps: Ka₁ >> Ka₂). It is a powerful dehydrating agent (chars carbohydrates). It is a moderate oxidising agent (oxidises Cu to CuSO₄, S to SO₂, C to CO₂, with SO₂ as the reduction product).

concepts.name: Group 17 Elements – General Introduction
concepts.explanation: Group 17 elements (F, Cl, Br, I, At) are called halogens (salt producers). They have the general electronic configuration ns²np⁵. They are highly reactive non-metals. Fluorine and chlorine are abundant; bromine and iodine are less so. Astatine is radioactive.

concepts.name: Group 17 – Electronic Configuration and Atomic Properties
concepts.explanation: Halogens have seven valence electrons (ns²np⁵) and are one electron short of a noble gas configuration. Atomic and ionic radii increase down the group. Ionisation enthalpy decreases down the group. Electron gain enthalpy: F is less negative than Cl due to small size and electron-electron repulsion.

concepts.name: Group 17 – Physical Properties
concepts.explanation: F₂ is yellow gas, Cl₂ is greenish-yellow gas, Br₂ is red liquid, I₂ is violet solid. Melting and boiling points increase down the group. All are coloured due to absorption of visible radiation. Halogens are soluble in organic solvents (coloured solutions). F₂ has anomalously low bond dissociation enthalpy.

concepts.name: Group 17 – Oxidation States
concepts.explanation: All halogens show -1 oxidation state. Cl, Br, I show +1, +3, +5, and +7 oxidation states using d-orbitals. F shows only -1 (no d-orbitals, most electronegative). Higher oxidation states occur in combination with O or F.

concepts.name: Anomalous Behaviour of Fluorine
concepts.explanation: F is anomalous due to its small size, highest electronegativity, low F-F bond dissociation enthalpy, and absence of d-orbitals. It forms only one oxoacid (HOF). HF is a liquid due to H-bonding. F₂ is the strongest oxidising agent.

concepts.name: Group 17 – Reactivity and Oxidising Power
concepts.explanation: Halogens are strong oxidising agents: F₂ > Cl₂ > Br₂ > I₂. F₂ oxidises water to O₂. Cl₂ and Br₂ react with water to give HX and HOX. I₂ reacts less readily. A halogen oxidises halide ions of higher atomic number: F₂ + 2X⁻ → 2F⁻ + X₂ (X = Cl, Br, I).
concepts.formula_reaction: 2F₂ + 2H₂O → 4H⁺ + 4F⁻ + O₂; X₂ + H₂O → HX + HOX (X = Cl, Br)

concepts.name: Hydrogen Halides (HX)
concepts.explanation: Halogens react with H₂ to form HX. Acidic strength: HF < HCl < HBr < HI. Thermal stability decreases down the group (H-F > H-Cl > H-Br > H-I). H-F is a liquid due to H-bonding; others are gases.

concepts.name: Chlorine (Cl₂) – Preparation
concepts.explanation: Cl₂ is prepared by: (i) MnO₂ + 4HCl → MnCl₂ + Cl₂ + 2H₂O; (ii) 2KMnO₄ + 16HCl → 2KCl + 2MnCl₂ + 8H₂O + 5Cl₂; (iii) Deacon's process: 4HCl + O₂ → 2Cl₂ + 2H₂O (CuCl₂ catalyst); (iv) electrolysis of brine.

concepts.name: Chlorine – Properties and Reactions
concepts.explanation: Cl₂ is a greenish-yellow gas with a pungent odour. It reacts with metals and non-metals to form chlorides. It has a high affinity for H₂ and displaces H₂ from compounds (H₂S, NH₃, hydrocarbons). With cold dilute NaOH: Cl₂ + 2NaOH → NaCl + NaOCl + H₂O; with hot concentrated NaOH: 3Cl₂ + 6NaOH → 5NaCl + NaClO₃ + 3H₂O.

concepts.name: Bleaching Action of Chlorine
concepts.explanation: Chlorine bleaches in the presence of moisture: Cl₂ + H₂O → 2HCl + [O]. The nascent oxygen oxidises the coloured substance to a colourless one. The bleaching action is permanent (oxidation).

concepts.name: Hydrogen Chloride (HCl) – Preparation and Properties
concepts.explanation: HCl is prepared by heating NaCl with conc. H₂SO₄: NaCl + H₂SO₄ → NaHSO₄ + HCl (420 K); NaHSO₄ + NaCl → Na₂SO₄ + HCl (823 K). HCl is a colourless, pungent gas, extremely soluble in water (strong acid). Aqua regia (HCl + HNO₃) dissolves noble metals (Au, Pt).
concepts.formula_reaction: NaCl + H₂SO₄ → NaHSO₄ + HCl; Au + 4H⁺ + NO₃⁻ + 4Cl⁻ → AuCl₄⁻ + NO + 2H₂O

concepts.name: Oxoacids of Halogens
concepts.explanation: Halogens form several oxoacids: HOX (hypohalous acid), HOXO (halous acid), HOXO₂ (halic acid), HOXO₃ (perhalic acid). Fluorine forms only HOF. Acid strength increases with the number of oxygen atoms: HOCl < HClO₂ < HClO₃ < HClO₄. HClO₄ is the strongest acid.

concepts.name: Interhalogen Compounds
concepts.explanation: Interhalogen compounds are formed by the reaction of two different halogens. Types: XX', XX'₃, XX'₅, XX'₇ (X is larger, X' is smaller halogen). They are more reactive than halogens (except F₂). They hydrolyse to give halide and oxoacid of the larger halogen. Uses: fluorinating agents (ClF₃, BrF₃ for UF₆ production).

concepts.name: Group 18 Elements – Noble Gases
concepts.explanation: Group 18 elements (He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn) have the general electronic configuration ns²np⁶ (except He: 1s²). They are colourless, odourless, tasteless monoatomic gases. Their inertness is due to completely filled valence shells, high ionisation enthalpies, and positive electron gain enthalpies.

concepts.name: Noble Gases – Occurrence and Uses
concepts.explanation: All noble gases except Rn occur in the atmosphere (Ar ~1% by volume). He also occurs in natural gas. Uses: He – balloons, cryogenics, diving apparatus diluent; Ne – discharge tubes, fluorescent bulbs; Ar – inert atmosphere in metallurgy, electric bulbs; Kr/Xe – special light bulbs.

concepts.name: Xenon Fluorides – Preparation
concepts.explanation: Xenon reacts directly with fluorine under different conditions: Xe + F₂ → XeF₂ (673 K, 1 bar, Xe excess); Xe + 2F₂ → XeF₄ (873 K, 7 bar, 1:5 ratio); Xe + 3F₂ → XeF₆ (573 K, 60-70 bar, 1:20 ratio). They are colourless crystalline solids and powerful fluorinating agents.
concepts.formula_reaction: Xe + F₂ → XeF₂; Xe + 2F₂ → XeF₄; Xe + 3F₂ → XeF₆

concepts.name: Xenon Fluorides – Structures
concepts.explanation: XeF₂ is linear (sp³d hybridisation, 3 lone pairs). XeF₄ is square planar (sp³d², 2 lone pairs). XeF₆ has a distorted octahedral structure (sp³d³, 1 lone pair). All structures are predicted by VSEPR theory.

concepts.name: Xenon Oxides and Oxyfluorides
concepts.explanation: XeO₃ is formed by hydrolysis of XeF₆: XeF₆ + 3H₂O → XeO₃ + 6HF. It is a colourless explosive solid (pyramidal structure). XeOF₄ and XeO₂F₂ are formed by partial hydrolysis. XeO₃ is explosive and has a pyramidal structure.
concepts.formula_reaction: XeF₆ + H₂O → XeOF₄ + 2HF; XeF₆ + 2H₂O → XeO₂F₂ + 4HF; XeF₆ + 3H₂O → XeO₃ + 6HF

concepts.name: Xenon Compounds – Reactions and Properties
concepts.explanation: Xenon fluorides react with fluoride acceptors to form cationic species: XeF₂ + PF₅ → [XeF]⁺[PF₆]⁻; XeF₄ + SbF₅ → [XeF₃]⁺[SbF₆]⁻. XeF₆ reacts with MF (M = Na, K, Rb, Cs) to form M⁺[XeF₇]⁻. All xenon compounds are powerful oxidising agents.
